narration: Imagine your dog walks up to a board of buttons… and suddenly one word changes everything. Is it real communication—or just a very cute trick?

under_narration.caption: a graph showing the number of people who have been diagnosed
a col of many different pictures of people

segments.transcript: [0.00s-0.48s] [SPEAKER_14] do know... | [0.84s-1.38s] [SPEAKER_00] Walk. | [1.40s-2.39s] [SPEAKER_14] ...thanks to buttons that | [2.39s-4.80s] [SPEAKER_14] that seem to give dogs the ability to talk.

[0.00s-1.47s] [SPEAKER_14] record different words on each | [1.47s-2.12s] [SPEAKER_14] each of the buttons. | [2.14s-2.93s] [SPEAKER_10] Stella. | [2.93s-3.06s] [SPEAKER_10] Stella. | [3.08s-5.91s] [SPEAKER_10] Stella is the world's first talking dog. | [5.91s-6.42s] [SPEAKER_10] dog. | [6.44s-7.72s] [SPEAKER_14] It's a big claim. | [8.65s-10.71s] [SPEAKER_14] So what makes Christina think it's true?

narration: The story starts small: a few buttons, a curious dog, and a human who decides to see what happens if words become toys.

under_narration.caption: a woman sitting on the floor playing with her dog
a woman playing with a dog on the floor

segments.transcript: [1.03s-4.33s] [SPEAKER_14] Christina got some buttons for Stella to see what would happen.

[0.00s-2.87s] [SPEAKER_10] of weeks, she didn't pay attention to the buttons at all. | [4.64s-9.76s] [SPEAKER_14] Even when Stella became interested in the buttons, it took a while before she managed to press one. | [11.98s-15.27s] [SPEAKER_04] You're so close, Stella. | [15.27s-15.51s] [SPEAKER_04] Stella. | [15.53s-16.85s] [SPEAKER_14] But finally, she cracked it. | [17.37s-20.33s] [SPEAKER_14] And the next morning, Christina caught it on camera. | [21.26s-22.24s] [SPEAKER_08] Let's go outside.

narration: But here’s the twist: pressing a button isn’t the same as understanding a word. A reward can make any behavior look like language.

segments.transcript: [0.85s-4.20s] [SPEAKER_14] Rohan has learned to associate an action, like pressing | [4.20s-7.28s] [SPEAKER_14] pressing a button, with an outcome, like getting a treat. | [7.96s-11.58s] [SPEAKER_14] He likes treats, so he keeps pressing the buttons. | [12.76s-16.37s] [SPEAKER_14] It's known as associative learning, or conditioning. | [17.48s-20.12s] [SPEAKER_14] But it doesn't mean he knows what the words treat, or | [20.12s-22.59s] [SPEAKER_14] thanks, or love you actually mean.
[0.00s-1.19s] [SPEAKER_03] It's just associative learning.

narration: And social media makes it even harder—because we mostly see the ‘wow’ moments, not the messy, random button mashing.

segments.transcript: [0.95s-3.80s] [SPEAKER_14] As news of Stella and her word buttons has spread, | [3.80s-3.87s] [SPEAKER_14] spread, | [5.27s-11.94s] [SPEAKER_14] Other so-called talking dogs have flooded social media with their seemingly amazing feats of communication. | [15.35s-17.28s] [SPEAKER_14] These dogs seem to be using our | [17.28s-17.87s] [SPEAKER_14] our words. | [20.46s-21.45s] [SPEAKER_14] So is that | [21.45s-22.94s] [SPEAKER_14] enough to say they're talking?

[0.00s-1.17s] [SPEAKER_15] accidents. | [1.18s-3.90s] [SPEAKER_14] If dog owners only post clips of their dogs pressing | [3.90s-6.21s] [SPEAKER_14] pressing word buttons that seem to make sense | [6.21s-11.11s] [SPEAKER_14] sense and ignore all the random presses, it would be completely misleading.

narration: So scientists ask a sharper question: if dogs really mean something, can we catch intention in the data—at scale?

under_narration.caption: a graph showing the number of people who have been diagnosed

segments.transcript: [0.00s-0.78s] [SPEAKER_14] Federico and | [0.78s-1.68s] [SPEAKER_14] Amalia have been recruiting | [1.68s-2.61s] [SPEAKER_14] recruiting participants | [2.61s-3.41s] [SPEAKER_14] participants from around | [3.41s-4.00s] [SPEAKER_14] around the world. | [7.16s-9.05s] [SPEAKER_14] While early contributors in the study used | [9.05s-11.98s] [SPEAKER_14] used simple spreadsheets to log their dogs' button presses, | [11.98s-15.63s] [SPEAKER_14] presses, many owners are now using buttons that automatically | [15.63s-18.78s] [SPEAKER_14] automatically record which words are pressed and when.

[2.93s-5.20s] [SPEAKER_14] Federico is finally starting to answer. | [5.23s-5.67s] [SPEAKER_14] He's | [5.67s-7.44s] [SPEAKER_14] He's gathered data | [7.44s-13.38s] [SPEAKER_14] data on the word buttons being used by an astonishing 10,000 dogs in almost 50 countries. | [14.72s-18.43s] [SPEAKER_14] It's the largest animal communication study in history.

narration: When you look across thousands of dogs, patterns appear. Some words rise to the top—because dogs keep choosing them.

segments.transcript: [16.14s-22.79s] [SPEAKER_14] Federico's analysis of over 10 million button presses by thousands of dogs over almost two years | [22.79s-25.97s] [SPEAKER_14] shows that these are the most popular words. | [27.48s-31.72s] [SPEAKER_14] Of course, all the words on sound boards are initially put there by humans. | [33.00s-35.82s] [SPEAKER_14] But these are the ones that the dogs are pressing most. | [37.14s-41.77s] [SPEAKER_14] And among these words, Federico has found that some are much more popular. | [42.55s-45.91s] [SPEAKER_14] The dogs are choosing to press them more than others.

[0.00s-1.40s] [SPEAKER_15] play, outside, food | [1.40s-4.63s] [SPEAKER_15] are the buttons that the dogs tend to press the most, which is | [4.63s-6.38s] [SPEAKER_15] is very much in line with being a dog. | [7.28s-11.35s] [SPEAKER_15] You can also see water, potty, treat, all done.

[0.00s-1.50s] [SPEAKER_14] fact that some words are used | [1.50s-6.14s] [SPEAKER_14] used more than others shows that across the population, the button | [6.14s-7.54s] [SPEAKER_14] button use isn't random.

narration: Still, a pattern isn’t proof. Maybe dogs just hit whatever’s closest—two buttons side by side—again and again.

under_narration.caption: a dog playing with a toy on the floor
a white dog standing on top of a wooden floor
a dog playing with a toy on the floor
a woman sitting on the floor playing with her dog

segments.transcript: [2.11s-4.03s] [SPEAKER_14] And what if the dogs are just pressing buttons | [4.03s-5.72s] [SPEAKER_14] buttons that are easy to reach? | [8.64s-12.91s] [SPEAKER_15] What can happen, of course, is that the dog might just end up pushing, for example, | [12.91s-15.71s] [SPEAKER_15] example, these two buttons that are close to each other | [15.71s-18.47s] [SPEAKER_15] just because they are adjacent to each other and it's just | [18.47s-19.47s] [SPEAKER_15] just easy for them.
[3.73s-5.30s] [SPEAKER_14] All that data can help to show | [5.30s-8.65s] [SPEAKER_14] show whether dog button use is random or intentional.

narration: To really test meaning, you have to remove the human hints: no tone of voice, no pointing, no accidental cues—just the word… and the dog’s choice.

under_narration.caption: a blue background with words in different languages

segments.transcript: [0.00s-2.53s] [SPEAKER_14] means the same thing to a dog as when that | [2.53s-4.51s] [SPEAKER_14] that same word is spoken by a person.

[6.73s-10.32s] [SPEAKER_14] because when we say words, we add all sorts of extra details. | [10.78s-13.44s] [SPEAKER_14] Pitch, tone, body language. | [13.46s-16.25s] [SPEAKER_09] You are the messiest drinker. | [16.25s-16.53s] [SPEAKER_09] drinker. | [16.54s-19.51s] [SPEAKER_15] But a word from a button is the same every time.

[2.89s-6.50s] [SPEAKER_14] The research team wore headphones and owners were blindfolded | [6.50s-11.24s] [SPEAKER_14] blindfolded to make sure neither could inadvertently give clues about which button had been pressed. | [11.24s-11.50s] [SPEAKER_14] pressed.

narration: And then comes the moment you’re waiting for: a button is pressed… and the dog does the matching thing—without being led.

under_narration.caption: a woman playing with a dog on the floor

segments.transcript: [12.13s-18.86s] [SPEAKER_15] And then you see the dog go straight for the toy basket, retrieves the ball, like, let's play. | [21.86s-24.12s] [SPEAKER_15] And so it's contextually appropriate with respect | [24.12s-26.33s] [SPEAKER_15] respect to what button had just been pressed.

[3.88s-5.97s] [SPEAKER_00] This is client trial number four. | [6.37s-12.95s] [SPEAKER_15] The experimenter is wearing headphones so that she could not hear the word that is produced once you push that button.

narration: But the real payoff isn’t ‘ball’ or ‘walk.’ The big leap is when a dog uses a word in a brand-new situation—like it’s a tool, not a trick.

under_narration.caption: a graph showing the number of people who have been diagnosed
a white dog standing on top of a wooden floor

segments.transcript: [0.96s-6.15s] [SPEAKER_14] Amalia, Federico's colleague, felt that use of the word help suggested greater | [6.15s-10.11s] [SPEAKER_14] cognitive abilities than most of the button words used by dogs.
[19.86s-21.29s] [SPEAKER_03] Help is interesting because | [21.29s-25.21s] [SPEAKER_03] it's a little bit more general than a lot of the simpler buttons that these dogs might have.

[4.31s-5.47s] [SPEAKER_03] She presses help, look, | [5.47s-7.05s] [SPEAKER_03] look, and then she looks to Sasha. | [8.15s-15.24s] [SPEAKER_14] Parker had never before been presented with this scenario, and so had never used the words look or help in this context. | [18.40s-20.48s] [SPEAKER_03] So because it's the very first time that they've seen this, it | [20.48s-21.38s] [SPEAKER_03] it tells us two things. | [21.42s-23.29s] [SPEAKER_03] It tells us that behavior wasn't trained, | [23.29s-27.83s] [SPEAKER_03] and it tells us that the dog is having to think about what this | [27.83s-30.93s] [SPEAKER_03] requires from them in terms of communication to their owner, and | [30.93s-34.20s] [SPEAKER_03] and that they need to generalize a button that they've not used in this context before.

narration: That’s the mini-mystery of button learning: some of it is simple conditioning, some of it might be meaning—and the best stories are the ones we can actually test.

under_narration.caption: a close up of a table with many bottles on it

segments.transcript: [35.66s-43.01s] [SPEAKER_14] It seems from this experiment that Parker understands that the word help can be used in a broad range of scenarios.

[2.57s-7.16s] [SPEAKER_14] We know that some dogs seem to make sense some of the time with their button presses. | [8.44s-9.24s] [SPEAKER_14] But the data that | [9.24s-13.27s] [SPEAKER_14] Federico is collecting will help his team understand the bigger picture. | [14.05s-14.52s] [SPEAKER_14] Do the thousands | [14.52s-17.75s] [SPEAKER_14] thousands of dogs taking part know what they're doing with buttons? | [18.51s-20.47s] [SPEAKER_14] Or is most of it just random?
